notable.notable_blurb: First woman Nobel laureate; first to win twice

cross_connections.display_label: the UNICEF chief who married her daughter Ève
the woman whose physics publishing she followed decades later

narrative_blocks.header: First woman Nobel laureate; first to win two
narrative_blocks.body: Marie Skłodowska-Curie won the 1903 Nobel Prize in Physics (shared with Pierre and Becquerel) and the 1911 Nobel Prize in Chemistry (sole) — the first woman ever awarded a Nobel, and the only person ever to win Nobel Prizes in two different sciences. The 1911 Chemistry prize recognized her discovery, isolation, and study of radium and polonium. Her daughter Irène and son-in-law Frédéric Joliot-Curie would win the 1935 Chemistry Nobel together — a four-Nobel chain extended to five when Eve's husband Henry Labouisse accepted the 1965 Peace Prize for UNICEF.

narrative_blocks.header: Took husband's Sorbonne chair after his death, 1906
narrative_blocks.body: After Pierre's death in 1906 Marie took over his Professor of General Physics position at the Sorbonne — the first time a woman held that chair. She continued radium research there and was appointed Director of the Curie Laboratory at the Radium Institute, founded in 1914. During the First World War she developed mobile X-ray units known as 'Little Curies' for use in French field hospitals.

Marie Curie died on July 4, 1934 of aplastic anemia — the result of decades of unshielded radiation exposure during her radium research. She was 66. Her notebooks remain radioactive and must be handled with lead-lined protection; she and Pierre were both reinterred under the Panthéon in 1995, Marie the first woman so honored.

notable.notable_blurb: Co-discovered radium and polonium; 1903 Nobel Physics

cross_connections.display_label: the UNICEF chief who married his daughter Ève

narrative_blocks.header: Shared 1903 Nobel Physics with Marie and Becquerel
narrative_blocks.body: Pierre Curie shared the 1903 Nobel Prize in Physics with his wife Marie Skłodowska-Curie and Antoine Henri Becquerel for their joint research on radioactivity. He and Marie discovered the elements polonium and radium while investigating uranium ores. Pierre deliberately exposed himself to radiation in 1900 to test its physiological effects.

On April 19, 1906 — three years after the Nobel — Pierre slipped on wet pavement at the Rue Dauphine in Paris and was run over by a horse-drawn cart. He was 46. The accident left Marie a widow with two young daughters; she took over his teaching position at the Sorbonne, the first woman to hold it.

In 1880 Pierre and his brother Jacques Curie demonstrated the direct piezoelectric effect — that mechanical stress on certain crystals generates an electric charge. The discovery underpins modern microphones, sonar, and quartz watches. Pierre was 21 at the time.

narrative_blocks.header: The only Curie without a Nobel Prize
narrative_blocks.body: Ève Curie was the younger daughter of Marie and Pierre Curie, and the only member of her family who did not become a scientist. She joked that she was the only Curie without a Nobel Prize — until 1965, when her husband Henry Labouisse accepted the Nobel Peace Prize on behalf of UNICEF, completing the family legacy of five Nobel laureates.

narrative_blocks.header: Her biography of Marie Curie won the National
narrative_blocks.body: Published in 1937, Ève's biography Madame Curie became an international bestseller and won the National Book Award. She followed it with Journey Among Warriors (1943), dispatches from the front lines of World War II that earned a Pulitzer nomination.

From the 1960s onward Ève devoted herself to UNICEF, traveling to over 100 countries with her husband, the organization's executive director. The United Nations Secretary General Kofi Annan visited her in her New York apartment on her 100th birthday in 2004. She died in Manhattan in 2007 at age 102.

sources.short: Online vital records; The Record LXXI; EH 1909

notable.notable_blurb: UNICEF director; accepted 1965 Nobel Peace Prize

cross_connections.display_label: Junior ECA Paris staffer on Marshall Plan, later Fitzgerald biographer
his father-in-law, who shared a Nobel for radioactivity
his mother-in-law, twice a Nobel laureate

narrative_blocks.body: Henry Labouisse became Executive Director of UNICEF in 1965 — the year UNICEF was awarded the Nobel Peace Prize. He accepted the prize on the organization's behalf. UNICEF would conduct major relief operations under his fourteen-year leadership, including during the Nigerian civil war in 1968 and in Cambodia in 1979.

narrative_blocks.header: Married Eve Curie; son-in-law of Marie Curie
narrative_blocks.body: His second marriage on November 19, 1954 to Ève Curie made him son-in-law of Pierre and Marie Curie. The Curie family won five Nobel Prizes — Pierre and Marie (Physics 1903), Marie again (Chemistry 1911), Irène and Frédéric Joliot-Curie (Chemistry 1935), and Henry himself accepting the Peace Prize for UNICEF in 1965. Ève survived him by twenty years, dying in 2007 at age 102.

narrative_blocks.header: Great-great-grandson of Confederate bishop Leonidas Polk
narrative_blocks.body: His mother Frances Devereux Huger Labouisse descended from Leonidas Polk (X00867) — the Episcopal bishop killed as a Confederate general at Pine Mountain in 1864. Polk's daughter Elizabeth Devereux Polk married William Elliott Huger; their daughter Frances was Henry's mother. The Curie marriage thus joined Henry's two genealogical headlines — Hooker-Edwards Puritan lineage on the New England side, Polk Confederate aristocracy on the Louisiana side.
